candidate_problem_record.subject: physics
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: {"context": "As shown in figure (a), four points A, B, M, and N in the same vertical plane are all at a distance of $\\sqrt{2}L$ from point $O$. $O$ is the midpoint of the horizontal line connecting A and B. M and N are on the perpendicular bisector of the line connecting A and B. Two point charges, each with a charge of $Q$ ($Q>0$), are fixed at points A and B, respectively. An $x$-axis is established with $O$ as the origin, and vertically downwards as the positive direction. If the electric potential at infinity is taken as zero, the variation of electric potential $\\varphi$ along ON with respect to position $x$ is shown in figure (b). A small ball $S_{1}$ with a charge of $Q$ ($Q>0$) falls vertically from point M with a certain initial kinetic energy, and passes through point N after a certain period. The magnitude of its acceleration $a$ during its motion in the ON segment varies with position $x$ as shown in figure (c). In the figures, $g$ is the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration, and $k$ is the electrostatic constant.", "sub_question_1": "Determine the magnitude of the electric force experienced by the small ball $S_1$ at point M.", "sub_question_2": "When the small ball $S_{1}$ moves to point N, it undergoes an elastic collision with an uncharged insulating small ball $S_{2}$ moving along the negative $x$-axis. It is known that the masses of $S_1$ and $S_{2}$ are equal, and the kinetic energy of $S_{1}$ is $\\frac{4k Q^{2}}{3L}$ before and after the collision. The collision time is extremely short. Determine the magnitude of the momentum of $S_{2}$ before the collision.", "sub_question_3": "Now, $S_{2}$ is fixed at point N. To ensure that $S_1$ can move to point N and collide with it, what condition must be satisfied by the initial kinetic energy of $S_1$ when it falls from point M?"}
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集被视为具备较稳定的多解潜力，可进入更强的多解挖掘链路。

sentence_segments.text: {"context": "As shown in figure (a), four points A, B, M, and N in the same vertical plane are all at a distance of $\\sqrt{2}L$ from point $O$.
$O$ is the midpoint of the horizontal line connecting A and B.
M and N are on the perpendicular bisector of the line connecting A and B.
Two point charges, each with a charge of $Q$ ($Q>0$), are fixed at points A and B, respectively.
An $x$-axis is established with $O$ as the origin, and vertically downwards as the positive direction.
If the electric potential at infinity is taken as zero, the variation of electric potential $\\varphi$ along ON with respect to position $x$ is shown in figure (b).
A small ball $S_{1}$ with a charge of $Q$ ($Q>0$) falls vertically from point M with a certain initial kinetic energy, and passes through point N after a certain period.
The magnitude of its acceleration $a$ during its motion in the ON segment varies with position $x$ as shown in figure (c).

In the figures, $g$ is the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration, and $k$ is the electrostatic constant.", "sub_question_1": "Determine the magnitude of the electric force experienced by the small ball $S_1$ at point M.", "sub_question_2": "When the small ball $S_{1}$ moves to point N, it undergoes an elastic collision with an uncharged insulating small ball $S_{2}$ moving along the negative $x$-axis.
It is known that the masses of $S_1$ and $S_{2}$ are equal, and the kinetic energy of $S_{1}$ is $\\frac{4k Q^{2}}{3L}$ before and after the collision.
The collision time is extremely short.
Determine the magnitude of the momentum of $S_{2}$ before the collision.", "sub_question_3": "Now, $S_{2}$ is fixed at point N.
To ensure that $S_1$ can move to point N and collide with it, what condition must be satisfied by the initial kinetic energy of $S_1$ when it falls from point M?"}

conditions.text: {"context": "As shown in figure (a), four points A, B, M, and N in the same vertical plane are all at a distance of $\\sqrt{2}L$ from point $O$.
Two point charges, each with a charge of $Q$ ($Q>0$), are fixed at points A and B, respectively.
If the electric potential at infinity is taken as zero, the variation of electric potential $\\varphi$ along ON with respect to position $x$ is shown in figure (b).
A small ball $S_{1}$ with a charge of $Q$ ($Q>0$) falls vertically from point M with a certain initial kinetic energy, and passes through point N after a certain period.
The magnitude of its acceleration $a$ during its motion in the ON segment varies with position $x$ as shown in figure (c).
It is known that the masses of $S_1$ and $S_{2}$ are equal, and the kinetic energy of $S_{1}$ is $\\frac{4k Q^{2}}{3L}$ before and after the collision.

M and N are on the perpendicular bisector of the line connecting A and B. Two point charges, each with a charge of $Q$ ($Q>0$), are fixed at points A and B, respectively. An $x$-axis is established with $O$ as the origin, and vertically downwards as the positive direction. If the electric potential at infinity is taken as zero, the variation of electric potential $\\varphi$ along ON with respect to position $x$ is shown in figure (b). A small ball $S_{1}$ with a charge of $Q$ ($Q>0$) falls vertically from point M with a certain initial kinetic energy, and passes through point N after a certain period. The magnitude of its acceleration $a$ during its motion in the ON segment varies with position $x$ as shown in figure (c). In the figures, $g$ is the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration, and $k$ is the electrostatic constant.", "sub_question_1": "Determine the magnitude of the electric force experienced by the small ball $S_1$ at point M.", "sub_question_2": "When the small ball $S_{1}$ moves to point N, it undergoes an elastic collision with an uncharged insulating small ball $S_{2}$ moving along the negative $x$-axis. It is known that the masses of $S_1$ and $S_{2}$ are equal, and the kinetic energy of $S_{1}$ is $\\frac{4k Q^{2}}{3L}$ before and after the collision. The collision time is extremely short. Determine the magnitude of the momentum of $S_{2}$ before the collision.", "sub_question_3": "Now, $S_{2}$ is fixed at point N. To ensure that $S_1$ can move to point N and collide with it, what condition must be satisfied by the initial kinetic energy of $S_1$ when it falls from point M?"}
